title: I tried to build a superhuman Figgie bot
desc: A build log: I wanted a superhuman self-play RL agent for Jane Street's card game Figgie. Here's everything I tried — encoding the order book as tokens, self-play with PPO (which beat a Bayesian heuristic), and reaching for Deep CFR to push further (which fought me the whole way).
text: build log · reinforcement learning i tried to build a superhuman figgie bot nerdsniped • june 2026 • ~15 min read • self-play rl · a work in progress i wanted to build a superhuman figgie bot . not a decent one — a genuinely scary one, the kind that sits down at jane street's little trading game and quietly takes everyone's chips. this is the build log of that attempt: the idea, the things i tried, the one that worked, the one that fought me, and an honest accounting of how far i actually got. (spoiler, so you can calibrate: not superhuman yet — but further than i expected, and i think i can see the rest of the road. ) why figgie? because it's a beautiful target. four players, a rigged 40-card deck, an open order book, and one hidden fact — the goal suit — that nobody is told but everybody can infer from the cards in their hand. you buy, sell, and post quotes in real time, and when the round ends the players holding the most of the goal suit split a pot. it's poker for people who like market microstructure: a tiny, fully-specified rulebook, a brutal amount of hidden information, and a reward you can compute exactly (chips at the end). if you wanted to hand-design an rl playground, it would look a lot like this. my one rule for myself: no human games, no hand-written strategy. the bot would have to learn everything — the inference, the trading, the bluffing — by playing millions of games against copies of itself. the whole thing lives in figgierl ; what follows is the order i actually tried things in. (if you want the algorithms below built up from first principles, i wrote a companion explainer — policy gradients all the way to ppo, grpo and cfr, derived on this exact game.) 146 discrete actions (buy / sell / quote / cancel) ~149k parameters in the baseline transformer 4 players · 40 cards · one hidden goal suit 1000s of games per training iteration, batched on gpu 1 know the game, and pick something to beat before you can build something superhuman you need to know what "human" even means here, so let me get the rules out of the way and then nail down two opponents to measure against. the deck is deliberately lopsided. of the four suits, one is the common suit with 12 cards, two have 10, and one has only 8. the goal suit is always the same-colour partner of the 12-card common suit — spades↔clubs, diamonds↔hearts — and you are never told which it is. at the end, everyone earns 10 chips per goal card they hold, and whoever holds the most splits a bonus pot (100 chips, or 120 when the goal happens to be the rare 8-card suit). each player antes 50 to play. here is the whole game in one move: you can't see the goal suit, but you can see your own ten cards, and that is a surprisingly strong signal — pointing the wrong way . if you were dealt a lot of one suit, that suit is probably the 12-card common one, which means its partner is the goal. the suit you have the most of is the suit you should suspect least . a careful player computes, from their hand, the posterior probability that each suit is the goal, turns that into a fair value per card, and trades toward it. what your hand whispers. posterior probability that a suit is the goal (and the resulting fair value of one card), as a function of how many of that suit you were dealt — computed from the game's exact prior. the slope is negative : holding more of a suit is evidence it is the abundant common suit, so its partner, not it, is the prize. this counter-intuitive inference is the entire skill of figgie, and it's what we're asking the network to rediscover. i needed yardsticks — something concrete to point the bot at — so i wrote two reference opponents from scratch: random — picks uniformly among legal non-pass moves. the floor. if my bot can't beat this, nothing else matters. tom — a bayesian heuristic that computes the fair value above, hits any quote that crosses it by a margin, and otherwise posts tight quotes around fair value. not optimal, but it plays the inference — it's roughly "a competent human who knows the trick." beating tom is the first sign of real skill. so here's my mental scoreboard for the whole project. beat random = the bot understands that cards have value. beat tom = it has learned the actual inference and can trade on it. beat tom by a lot , and stay unbeatable when opponents adapt = the superhuman dream. everything below is me climbing that ladder. 2 attempt 0: make figgie speak transformer before any learning could happen i had to decide how the bot even sees the game. a game of figgie is a sequence of events: a deal, then a stream of public actions — someone posts a bid, someone lifts an offer, a trade prints. that is already shaped like language, so i treated it like one. each player sees a token stream : the first token encodes their private opening hand, and every subsequent token encodes one public order-book event. a causal transformer (pre-norm self-attention interleaved with swiglu mlp blocks) reads the stream and, at each step, emits two things: a distribution over the next action and a scalar estimate of how many chips the player expects to walk away with. the action space is a flat menu of 146 discrete moves : pass; buy or sell each of the four suits at the current best quote; post a bid or an ask for a suit at one of 16 discrete price levels; cancel a resting quote; or mass-cancel. illegal moves (selling a card you don't hold, quoting through your own resting order) are removed with an action mask before the softmax, so the policy only ever spends probability on legal actions. the setup. top: the opening hand plus the running order-book events become a token sequence; a causal transformer maps it to a masked policy over 146 actions and a value estimate, with a kv-cache so rollouts stay cheap. bottom: the self-play loop — the current policy π sits at a table with three opponents θ sampled from a frozen pool of its own past snapshots, thousands of games run in parallel on the gpu, end-of-game chips become the (zero-sum) reward, and a ppo or deep-cfr update produces the next weights, which periodically re-enter the pool. 3 the engine: self-play on the gpu, against your own ghosts with the encoding settled, i needed a way to generate experience. my self-imposed rule — no human games — means the agent learns purely from itself. the core loop: take the current policy, seat it at a table with three opponents drawn from a pool of frozen past snapshots of itself, play a huge batch of games, and update on the results. sampling old snapshots rather than always playing the very latest weights is the standard trick to keep self-play from chasing its own tail into a degenerate strategy — you have to stay good against a distribution of past selves, not just today's. the reward is simply the chips you finish with, which makes the game (nearly) zero-sum across the four seats. to make millions of games affordable, the whole environment is re-implemented as a batched, vectorised engine that steps thousands of games at once on the gpu, with a kv-cache so each agent move is a single-token forward pass rather than a re-encode of the whole history. a readable single-game cpu implementation is kept alongside it purely as the reference oracle for tests. why this is harder than it looks figgie is simultaneous-ish, imperfect-information, multi-agent, and zero-sum — the exact cocktail where naïve policy gradients can spin forever without converging. "beat a random opponent" falls quickly; "beat an opponent who is also adapting" is a moving target; "play a nash-ish strategy that can't be exploited" is the hard version. that ladder is exactly what the scorecard below measures. 4 attempt 1: ppo, the workhorse — and the first real milestone for the first serious attempt i reached for the boring, reliable option: ppo (clipped policy-gradient with gae), the workhorse of single-agent rl, pointed at the self-play loop. it is technically the "wrong" tool — figgie is an imperfect-information game and ppo has no special machinery for that — but it's robust and i wanted a signal that the whole pipeline worked before getting fancy. it worked better than i had any right to expect. within minutes the bot blew past random, and — the moment i'd actually been waiting for — it kept climbing and crossed tom , the bayesian heuristic, into positive territory: my best ppo runs finish at roughly +12 to +27 chips per game against tom and as much as +74 against random . a from-scratch transformer, fed nothing but chips, had reinvented the §1 inference well enough to out-trade a hand-written player that knows the trick. that's milestone one on the ladder, and it felt great. the scoreboard for my two attempts. solid lines are score vs tom; dotted are vs random (higher is better throughout). the one to watch right now is ppo : its solid line climbs above zero, meaning it beats the heuristic. the orange lines are my next attempt, deep cfr — hold that thought for §5. the x-axis is normalised training progress so the differently-sized runs overlay. so why not just declare victory and scale ppo up? because "beats tom by +20" is a long way from superhuman . ppo self-play has a known failure mode in these games: it can settle into a strategy that's good on average but exploitable — beatable by an opponent that adapts specifically to it. to chase the unbeatable version, i wanted the algorithm that was actually designed for hidden-information games. which brings me to the part that humbled me. 5 attempt 2: deep cfr, reaching for "unexploitable" deep cfr is the deep-learning descendant of counterfactual regret minimisation — the algorithm family that solved heads-up poker. unlike ppo it comes with a convergence story toward a nash-ish, hard-to-exploit strategy, which is exactly the property i want for a superhuman bot. on paper it's the right tool. in practice it spent a while reminding me that "on paper" does a lot of work. the first cfr run literally scored worse than random — the regret targets exploded and the policy diverged. getting it to even train took a checklist of fixes: scaling the reward down, clipping regrets and gradients to sane ranges, and forcing a healthy exploration floor so the strategy networks saw enough of the game. only after all that did it start behaving. and once it behaved, it learned — just not far enough, yet. over ~12k steps (about 27 minutes on one gpu) it climbs to a solid +25 to +30 chips against random and holds there, while against tom it claws upward from about −58 to the −30s but never crosses zero . so my fancier, theoretically-better attempt currently sits behind my boring one. that's the honest state of things. deep cfr, chips won per game. against random it goes solidly positive within a few thousand steps and stays. against tom it improves by ~20 chips but never reaches break-even — it's learning the right things, just not enough of them in the runs i've done so far. the reassuring part: this isn't a broken-self-play problem, it's a not-done-yet problem. the training health looks fine — mean reward sits near zero (as it should in a zero-sum game against your own snapshots) and the win rate climbs past the 0.25 line that four equal players start at , up to ~0.37, so the live policy really is outgrowing its frozen ghosts. the huge reward variance (a single deal swings ±60 chips) is just the cost of a card game, and the reason every update digests thousands of games. self-play health (deep cfr run). mean reward (orange, ±1 std band) rises from −40 toward the zero-sum equilibrium against the snapshot pool; win rate (purple, right axis) climbs from the 0.20s through the 0.25 four-player floor to ~0.37. healthy training — the ceiling is run length and tuning, not a sick optimiser. 6 so… am i superhuman yet? no. but here's the scoreboard, honestly tallied. positive vs-random means "better than a random player"; positive vs-tom means "better than a competent bayesian human-ish player": agent vs random (best) vs tom (best) win rate verdict random 0 (by def.) — 0.25 the floor tom (heuristic) — 0 (by def.) — the bar ppo self-play +74.0 +12 to +27 — clears the bar ✓ deep-cfr self-play +31.5 −31.7 0.37 beats floor, chasing bar what i've actually got is a bot that convincingly beats a random player and out-trades a solid heuristic , learned entirely from self-play with no human games — which honestly delights me — and a clear, unfinished road to "superhuman" from here. the §1 inference, the thing that is figgie, emerged from chips alone. that's the headline i'm keeping. the thing the project taught me the most-cited fact i walked away with isn't about figgie, it's about rl engineering: my theoretically better algorithm (deep cfr) lost, for now, to my boring reliable one (ppo) — purely because cfr was harder to stabilise and i gave it shorter runs. pedigree on paper means nothing until the reward scaling, clipping, exploration, and run length are right. the algorithm choice was the easy decision; the engineering around it was the whole game. 7 what i'm trying next the road to superhuman, as i currently see it: let deep cfr cook. longer runs with the stabilisers properly tuned, to see whether its unexploitability story finally overtakes ppo once it has the time poker-scale cfr usually needs. harden against exploitation. a bigger, more diverse opponent pool — and ideally an explicit exploitability check — so "wins on average" becomes "can't be picked apart by an adapting opponent." open the hood. probe the value head to see whether the transformer has genuinely reconstructed the goal-suit posterior internally, or is winning via some shortcut that merely correlates with it. that tells me whether i'm building a trader or a lookup table. then, the real test: put it in front of strong human figgie players and find out how much of "beats my heuristic" survives contact with people who bluff back. 📺 nerdsniped on youtube this post is the written companion to nerdsniped — deepdives into mathsy side quests. if you'd rather watch the figgie bot get nerd-sniped into existence than read about it, the channel is here . reproducibility. everything is from-scratch pytorch: a causal transformer (pre-norm attention + swiglu) with a masked 146-action policy head and a scalar value head, trained by self-play with a frozen snapshot pool, optimised with muon + adam. the batched gpu engine vectorises thousands of figgie games at once; a readable single-game cpu implementation serves as the test oracle. two reference opponents — uniform-random and a bayesian-fair-value heuristic (tom) — are the benchmarks; scores are mean chips per game. curves are read directly from the experiments' tensorboard logs (deep-cfr run cfr_run2_fixed , 12k steps; legacy ppo run v29 ); the fair-value figure is computed from the game's exact deal prior. each run is fully specified by a config.json that re-creates its architecture and every hyperparameter.

title: From the policy gradient theorem to PPO, GRPO & CFR
desc: A self-contained guided derivation: from the policy gradient theorem to PPO, GRPO and CFR, taught on Figgie self-play. The theory companion to the FiggieRL build log.
text:
explainer · reinforcement learning from the policy gradient theorem to ppo, grpo & cfr nerdsniped • june 2026 • ~45 min read • a guided derivation everything you need to fully understand the three algorithms that matter for training a figgie agent by self-play — built up from first principles, one idea at a time, with every formula cashed out on the actual game. the theory companion to the figgierl build log . ⏱ ~45 minute read 📐 derivations included, collapsible 🎯 goal: ppo · grpo · cfr 🃏 grounded in self-play figgie you already know mdps, the bellman equations, value iteration and q-learning. we pick up exactly where that leaves off: those methods learn values and read a policy off them. here we learn the policy directly . by the end you'll see ppo, grpo and cfr as three answers to one question — how do you safely improve a policy from samples? contents the figgie testbed why policies, not values the policy gradient theorem reinforce & baselines actor–critic & gae ppo grpo counterfactual regret min. deep cfr choosing an algorithm references section 1 the figgie testbed every algorithm below is explained on one running problem: train a neural network to play figgie well, using only games it plays against copies of itself. before any rl, we need the game pinned down as an environment. the game in one paragraph figgie is jane street's trading card game. a 40-card deck has four suits dealt in counts {12, 10, 10, 8} — but you don't know which suit got which count. the 12-card suit fixes the goal suit : it is the other suit of the same colour (♠–♣ are black, ♦–♥ are red). the goal suit therefore has 8 or 10 cards. four players are dealt 10 cards each, ante \$50 into a \$200 pot, then trade single cards in an open continuous double auction for a few minutes — anyone may post a bid or an ask, or hit a standing quote. when time expires, each goal-suit card pays \$10, and whoever holds the most goal-suit cards takes the remainder of the pot. your edge comes from inferring the goal suit faster than the table and buying it cheap. 🃏 why figgie is a great rl teaching domain it hits every hard case at once: sparse reward (you only learn who won at the end), imperfect information (you never see the other hands — this is what makes cfr relevant), multi-agent non-stationarity (your opponents are also learning), and a large structured action space (post/cancel/lift quotes across four suits at sixteen prices). ppo and grpo will tackle the first three; cfr speaks directly to the second. casting it as an mdp (from the learner's seat) we train one network. from its seat, the other three players are part of the environment. concretely, matching the figgie/ implementation: mdp element in figgierl state \(s_t\) the full history this seat has observed: its 10-card opening hand plus the running sequence of public market events (quotes posted, trades executed). the network is a causal transformer over this token sequence — the tokens tensor of shape [b, t, 96] . the sequence is the state; attention is the memory. action \(a_t\) one of 146 discrete actions : pass , buy(suit) , sell(suit) , quote_bid(suit, price) , quote_ask(suit, price) over 4 suits × 16 prices, plus cancels and mass_cancel . illegal actions are removed each step by a boolean valid_action_mask before the softmax. policy \(\pi_	heta(a\mid s)\) the transformer's masked softmax head: a categorical distribution over the 146 actions, conditioned on the whole sequence so far. reward \(r_t\) the seat's chip delta at that step: cash paid/received on a trade, and at the final step the goal-suit payout. everyone antes \(-\$50\) up front ( chips = -50 ). it is sparse and end-loaded — most steps give \(0\), the score lands at the end. episode \(	au\) one game: up to max_actions ≈ 100 decision steps, then score_games settles the pot. return \(r(	au)\) net chips at game end — the seat's profit. this is the thing we want to maximise in expectation. ⚠ it is really a pomdp you never observe the opponents' hands or the true goal suit, so the "state" is a history , not a markov snapshot of the world. we handle this the standard deep-rl way — feed the whole observed history to a sequence model and let it learn a sufficient statistic (a belief state) in its hidden activations. keep this in your back pocket: it is precisely the gap that cfr (§8) addresses head-on with information sets instead of papering over with a recurrent/attention encoder. what "self-play" means here there is no fixed opponent to learn against; the environment's difficulty must rise with the learner. figgierl fills the other three seats from a pool : heuristic experts ( tomplayer , which prices each suit at a fair value derived from \(p(	ext{goal}\mid 	ext{hand})\); randomplayer ) plus frozen snapshots of the learner's own past weights , saved every snapshot_every iterations. training against a distribution of past selves is what stops the policy from chasing one exploitable opponent and forgetting how to play in general. ✦ the one objective behind everything let \(\pi_	heta\) be our policy with parameters \(	heta\). every algorithm in this document is a different way to push \(	heta\) up the same hill: \[ j(	heta) \;=\; \mathbb{e}_{	au \sim \pi_	heta}\! ig[\,r(	au)\, ig] \;=\; \mathbb{e}_{	au \sim \pi_	heta}\! ig[	extstyle\sum_{t} \gamma^{t} r_t ig]. \] we want \(	heta^\star=\arg\max_	heta j(	heta)\): the weights whose games earn the most chips on average. the trajectory distribution \(	au\sim\pi_	heta\) depends on \(	heta\) — that dependence is the whole technical difficulty, and the next section is about getting a gradient through it. section 2 why learn a policy, not a value you already know the value-based recipe: learn \(q^\star(s,a)\), then act greedily, \(\pi(s)=\arg\max_a q(s,a)\). it is beautiful when it works. figgie breaks three of its assumptions, and seeing exactly how motivates everything that follows. 1. the action space is large and structured. a \(\max_a q(s,a)\) needs a value for all 146 actions every step , and the greedy argmax gives a brittle, deterministic trader. worse, in a betting/trading game a deterministic policy is exploitable : opponents learn you always lift the ask at \$8 and front-run you. we will need stochastic policies, which the argmax fundamentally is not. 2. imperfect information demands randomisation. this is the deep one. in games where you hide information, the optimal strategy is often genuinely mixed — like bluffing in poker. there is no deterministic best response to randomise against; the nash strategy assigns positive probability to several actions in the same situation. a value function plus argmax cannot represent such a policy. a parameterised distribution \(\pi_	heta(a\mid s)\) can. (hold this thought for §8.) 3. we want to optimise the thing we care about, directly. value methods optimise a surrogate (bellman error) and hope a good policy falls out. policy-gradient methods differentiate \(j(	heta)\) — expected chips — and step \(	heta\) straight up that gradient. the objective you train is the objective you want. 💡 the reframing value iteration asks "how good is each state/action?" and acts greedily. policy gradients ask " nudge the knobs \(	heta\) so that whatever i just did, if it paid off, becomes more likely. " no bootstrapped value estimate is required to define the update — though, as we'll see, a value estimate is the single best tool for making that update less noisy. that tension — pure policy gradient vs. a helper critic — is the through-line from reinforce to ppo. section 3 the policy gradient theorem we want \(
abla_	heta j(	heta)\). the obstacle is that the expectation is taken over a distribution that itself depends on \(	heta\): change the weights and you change which games get played. you cannot just push the gradient inside the expectation. the fix is a one-line identity that the entire field rests on. the log-derivative ("score function") trick for any distribution \(p_	heta(x)\) and function \(f\), use \(
abla_	heta p_	heta = p_	heta 
abla_	heta \log p_	heta\): \[ 
abla_	heta\, \mathbb{e}_{x\sim p_	heta}[f(x)] = 
abla_	heta\!\!\int p_	heta(x) f(x)\,dx = \int p_	heta(x)\,
abla_	heta\!\log p_	heta(x)\, f(x)\,dx = \mathbb{e}_{x\sim p_	heta}\! ig[ f(x)\,
abla_	heta \log p_	heta(x) ig]. \] this converts "gradient of an expectation" — which we can't sample — into "expectation of a gradient", which we can : play games, evaluate the bracket on each, average. now apply it with \(x=	au\) and \(f=r\). full derivation: from \(j(	heta)\) to the score-function estimator a trajectory's probability factorises along the chain. with environment dynamics \(p(s_{t+1}\mid s_t,a_t)\) and initial state \(ho(s_0)\): \[ p_	heta(	au) = ho(s_0)\prod_{t=0}^{t-1} \pi_	heta(a_t\mid s_t)\,p(s_{t+1}\mid s_t,a_t). \] take logs — products become sums: \[ \log p_	heta(	au) = \logho(s_0) + \sum_{t} \log \pi_	heta(a_t\mid s_t) + \sum_{t}\log p(s_{t+1}\mid s_t,a_t). \] differentiate w.r.t. \(	heta\). the dynamics and the initial distribution do not depend on \(	heta\) , so their gradients vanish — we never need a model of the environment : \[ 
abla_	heta \log p_	heta(	au) = \sum_{t} 
abla_	heta \log \pi_	heta(a_t\mid s_t). \] substitute into the score-function identity with \(f(	au)=r(	au)\): \[  oxed{\;
abla_	heta j(	heta) = \mathbb{e}_{	au\sim\pi_	heta}\! ig[\, r(	au)\,	extstyle\sum_{t}
abla_	heta \log \pi_	heta(a_t\mid s_t) ig].\;} \] ✦ the policy gradient theorem \[ 
abla_	heta j(	heta) \;=\; \mathbb{e}_{	au\sim\pi_	heta}\! igg[\, \sum_{t=0}^{t-1} 
abla_	heta \log\pi_	heta(a_t\mid s_t)\;\underbrace{ ig(\sum_{t'\ge t}\gamma^{\,t'-t} r_{t'} ig)}_{	ext{return-to-go } \;\hat a_t 	ext{ (raw)}} igg]. \] two refinements are already baked in versus the boxed version above. (1) causality : an action at time \(t\) can only influence future reward, so we replace the whole-episode return \(r(	au)\) by the return-to-go \(g_t=\sum_{t'\ge t}\gamma^{t'-t}r_{t'}\). (2) that weight in front of each \(
abla\log\pi\) is the prototype of what we'll soon call the advantage . reading the formula each term is \(
abla_	heta \log\pi_	heta(a_t\mid s_t)\) scaled by a number. the gradient \(
abla\log\pi_	heta(a_t\mid s_t)\) points in parameter space in the direction that makes action \(a_t\) more probable in state \(s_t\). multiply by the return-to-go and sum: action led to a positive outcome (\(g_t>0\)) → step toward making it more likely. action led to a negative outcome (\(g_t<0\)) → step away , making it less likely. magnitude scales with how good/bad. that's the whole idea: reinforce what paid, suppress what cost — weighted by how much. 🃏 on the board mid-game your network posts quote_bid(♥, 7) — a bid of \$7 for a heart. the game plays out and that seat ends +\$40 . the update nudges \(	heta\) so that, in sequences that look like that one, \(\pi_	heta\) puts more mass on bidding \$7 for hearts. notice the catch that the rest of this document exists to fix: the +\$40 is credited to every action in the game, including the irrelevant pass es and a possibly bad heart bid that just got bailed out by good luck later. the raw signal is correct on average but drowning in noise. section 4 reinforce & the variance problem turn the theorem into an algorithm by replacing the expectation with a sample average over games you actually play. that's reinforce (williams, 1992) — the monte-carlo policy gradient. # reinforce on figgie self-play (one iteration) for iteration in range(num_iters): batch = [] for g in range(n): # play n self-play games traj = play_game( policy , opponents=sample_pool()) batch.append(traj) loss = 0 for traj in batch: g = discounted_returns_to_go(traj.rewards, gamma) # g_t for each step for (s, a, g_t) in zip(traj.states, traj.actions, g): loss = loss - log_prob ( policy , s, a) * g_t # ascent ⇒ negate loss = loss / n loss. backward (); optimizer. step () # θ ← θ + α·∇j in practice: a surrogate loss, then a normal optimizer a natural question at this point: when you implement reinforce, do you compute the gradient \(
abla_	heta j\) by hand and apply it, or do you have a loss function and a normal optimizer? almost always the latter. you build a scalar "surrogate loss" whose automatic-differentiation gradient equals the policy gradient, then call .backward() and a standard adam / sgd step. you essentially never compute \(
abla\log\pi\) by hand. the mechanism: optimizer.step() wants a scalar to differentiate, not a gradient. so construct the scalar whose derivative is the policy gradient. because the return \(g_t\) is a constant (a number from the environment), the loss \[ l(	heta) = - rac{1}{n}\sum_t \log\pi_	heta(a_t\mid s_t)\cdot g_t \qquad\longrightarrow\qquad 
abla_	heta l = - rac{1}{n}\sum_t 
abla_	heta\log\pi_	heta(a_t\mid s_t)\cdot g_t = -\,\widehat{
abla_	heta j} \] has a gradient equal to the negated policy gradient. minimising \(l\) with a normal optimizer therefore ascends \(j\) — the minus sign is only there because optimizers minimise. # reinforce, the way it's actually written (pytorch) returns = discounted_returns_to_go(rewards, gamma) # g_t — plain numbers returns = (returns - returns.mean()) / (returns.std() + 1e-8 ) # baseline / normalise logits = policy (states) # fresh forward pass dist = categorical(logits=logits) logp = dist. log_prob (actions) # log π_θ(a_t|s_t), differentiable loss = -(logp * returns. detach ()).mean() # the surrogate scalar optimizer. zero_grad () loss. backward () # autodiff produces the policy gradient optimizer. step () # a completely normal adam/sgd step ⚠ two things that trip everyone up 1. the loss value is meaningless. this is not a supervised loss you drive toward zero. its number, \(-	ext{mean}(\log\pi\cdot g)\), drifts up, down, or sideways while training succeeds — only its gradient carries information. don't watch loss.item() as a health signal; watch reward. 2. the weight must be detach() -ed. \(g_t\) (or, in actor–critic, the advantage) is a coefficient , not something to backprop through. forget to detach a learned advantage and gradients leak into the critic, and the expression is no longer the policy gradient. this is exactly the new_values.detach() in your repo's ppo_loss_batched — and the same class of mistake as the entropy-detach bug in the project notes. 💡 why not just code the gradient directly? you could — \(

siblings. once you see "it's all about how you compute \(\hat a_t\) and how hard you clip \(r_t\)," every one of these algorithms becomes a two-line variation on the policy gradient theorem from §3. figgierl is a hybrid — and seeing why is instructive the repo isn't dogmatically one or the other. it keeps a value head and gae (ppo-style fine-grained credit assignment across the ~100 trading decisions in a game) and carries group_size = 8 with seat-rotated groups (grpo-style structure that cancels deal luck), normalising returns across the batch. that's a deliberate engineering choice: per-step credit matters in figgie because a single mispriced quote 80 steps before the end genuinely is a localised mistake — pure outcome-level grpo would smear blame across the whole game. the group structure is layered on top to kill the dominant variance source (the deal). understanding both algorithms is what lets you read that design as the considered compromise it is, rather than a muddle. section 8 · key algorithm counterfactual regret minimization (cfr) ppo and grpo are gradient methods: they nudge a policy uphill and hope. they have no built-in notion that figgie is a game of hidden information against adversaries , and nothing guarantees they converge to an unexploitable strategy — left alone, two self-playing ppo agents can chase each other in circles. cfr comes from a different lineage — game theory — and was the engine behind superhuman poker (libratus, pluribus). it is built from the ground up for exactly figgie's defining feature: imperfect information. this section is the conceptual payoff; it requires a small shift in formalism. new formalism: extensive-form games and information sets drop the mdp picture; adopt the extensive-form game (a game tree). the key new object is the information set \(i\): the set of game states (tree nodes) that a player cannot tell apart from what they've observed. 🃏 an information set in figgie you hold {3♥, 2♦, …} and have watched a certain sequence of quotes and trades. many true world-states are consistent with that — every possible arrangement of the 30 cards you can't see, every possible goal suit not yet ruled out. all those distinct tree nodes form one information set \(i\) : the situations you experience as identical. a strategy can only condition on \(i\), never on the hidden truth — so it must assign one distribution over actions per information set . (this is the honest version of the pomdp hand-wave from §1: where ppo encodes the history into a vector and hopes, cfr reasons over the information set explicitly.) a strategy \(\sigma_i\) maps each info set \(i\) to a distribution \(\sigma_i(i)\) over its legal actions. the solution concept is a nash equilibrium : a strategy profile where no player can gain by unilaterally deviating — i.e. an unexploitable way to play. cfr is an algorithm that provably converges to one in two-player zero-sum games. the engine: regret matching forget gradients. cfr is built on regret : "how much better would i have done, in hindsight, had i always played action \(a\) here instead of my actual mixed strategy?" the algorithm is a loop: play the current strategy through the tree. at every information set, compute the regret of each action — counterfactually. accumulate those regrets over all iterations. set the next strategy proportional to positive accumulated regret . actions you wish you'd played more get more probability. counterfactual value "counterfactual" is the load-bearing word. the counterfactual value of info set \(i\) for player \(i\) weights each reachable terminal \(z\) by the probability that everyone else (and chance) plays to reach it — but treats player \(i\) as if they deliberately aimed to reach \(i\) (its own reach probability is factored out). this isolates "how good is being here, and what can i do about it," independent of how likely \(i\)'s own past play made reaching \(i\): \[ v_i(\sigma, i) \;=\; \sum_{z\in z_i} \pi^\sigma_{-i}(z)\,\pi^\sigma(z[i]\!	o\! z)\,u_i(z), \] where \(\pi^\sigma_{-i}\) is the reach probability from everyone but \(i\), \(u_i(z)\) the payoff (chips), and \(z_i\) the terminals through \(i\). counterfactual regret & the update the counterfactual regret of action \(a\) at \(i\) on iteration \(t\) is how much more counterfactual value you'd have gotten by committing to \(a\): \[ r^t(i,a) \;=\; v_i ig(\sigma^t_{i	o a},\,i ig) - v_i ig(\sigma^t, i ig), \qquad r^t(i,a) = \sum_{t=1}^{t} r^t(i,a). \] then regret matching sets the next strategy proportional to positive cumulative regret (uniform if nothing is positive): \[ \sigma^{t+1}(i,a) =  rac{r^{t,+}(i,a)}{\sum_b r^{t,+}(i,b)}, \qquad r^{+}=\max(r,0). \] ⚠ the strategy that converges is the average, not the last one this trips everyone up. the per-iteration strategies \(\sigma^t\) oscillate forever and do not individually approach equilibrium. the object that converges to nash is the average strategy \( ar\sigma^t(i,a)\propto\sum_t \pi_i^{\sigma^t}(i)\,\sigma^t(i,a)\) — the running, reach-weighted average of all strategies played. you must accumulate two tables per info set: cumulative regret (drives the next strategy) and the cumulative strategy (averaged at the end to read off the answer). why minimising regret yields nash (the one theorem to remember) regret matching is a no-regret online-learning rule: at each info set, average regret \( rac1t\sum_t r^t(i,a)	o 0\) as \(t	o\infty\) (it's bounded by \(o(1/\sqrt t)\)). the counterfactual construction's key lemma is that a player's total regret is bounded by the sum of their per-information-set counterfactual regrets — so minimising regret locally minimises it globally. finally, a folk theorem of online learning: in a two-player zero-sum game, if both players have average regret \(	o 0\), their average strategies form a nash equilibrium (the average exploitability is bounded by the sum of the two average regrets). chain those three facts and you have cfr's guarantee. a concrete pass on micro-figgie 🃏 one regret-matching update, by hand strip figgie to a toy: at some information set \(i\) you may buy ♥ , sell ♥ , or pass . start uniform, \(\sigma^1=(	frac13,	frac13,	frac13)\). you traverse the tree and find the counterfactual values \(v(	ext{buy})=+6\), \(v(	ext{sell})=-2\), \(v(	ext{pass})=0\), so the strategy's value is \(	frac13(6-2+0)=+1.33\). the instantaneous regrets are \(r(	ext{buy})=6-1.33=+4.67\), \(r(	ext{sell})=-2-1.33=-3.33\), \(r(	ext{pass})=0-1.33=-1.33\). only buy has positive cumulative regret, so σ² = (buy 1.0, sell 0.0, pass 0.0) — pure buy next iteration. next iteration the world (and opponents) shift, buying ♥ may now be regretted, and the mix swings back. over thousands of iterations the regrets balance and the average strategy settles into a stable, mixed, unexploitable way to trade hearts in that situation — perhaps (buy 0.55, pass 0.35, sell 0.10) . that mixing is the bluff-like randomisation §2 promised you couldn't get from an argmax. making cfr practical: sampling & variants vanilla cfr traverses the entire game tree every iteration — impossible for full figgie (a continuous double auction has an astronomically large tree). the fixes: monte-carlo cfr (mccfr). sample trajectories instead of full traversal. external sampling samples chance and opponents' actions, enumerating only the current player's; outcome sampling samples a single trajectory. unbiased estimates of counterfactual regret, dramatically cheaper. cfr+. floor cumulative regrets at zero ( regret-matching+ ) and weight the strategy average linearly toward later iterations. converges much faster in practice; powered the poker breakthroughs. abstraction. bucket similar information sets (similar hands/market states) into one, solve the smaller game, map back. necessary to fit a game like figgie into memory at all. deep cfr. replace the giant regret/strategy tables with neural networks that generalise across information sets — closing the loop back toward the function approximators ppo uses, but trained on the regret objective. cfr vs. the policy-gradient family ppo / grpo (policy gradient) cfr (regret minimisation) origin stochastic optimisation game theory / online learning optimises expected return \(j(	heta)\) (counterfactual) regret → 0 handles hidden info implicitly (encode history, hope) explicitly (information sets) convergence guarantee local optimum of \(j\); none vs. adaptive foe nash equilibrium (2p zero-sum) output strategy the current network the average strategy scales to huge state via neural nets (native) sampling + abstraction (or deep cfr) ✦ cfr in one breath track, at every information set, how much you regret not having favoured each action; play next in proportion to positive cumulative regret; and report the time-average strategy. in two-player zero-sum games that average provably converges to an unexploitable nash equilibrium — giving you, unlike a raw policy gradient, a guarantee that no opponent can systematically beat your figgie play. section 9 · key algorithm deep cfr: cfr meets function approximation tabular cfr has a fatal scaling problem on a game like figgie, and deep cfr (brown, lerer, gross & sandholm, 2019) is the fix that finally lets cfr's equilibrium guarantee ride on the same neural machinery that ppo uses. this chapter is where the two halves of the document — game-theoretic regret minimisation and deep function approximation — finally fuse. the wall tabular cfr hits recall §8: cfr keeps, for every information set \(i\), two tables — cumulative regret \(r(i,a)\) and the cumulative strategy. that is fine for toy games. full figgie is hopeless: an information set is "your hand + the entire observed market history." the number of distinct hands alone is enormous, and each is multiplied by every possible sequence of quotes and trades you could have watched. you would need to visit each information set many times to estimate its regret — but the vast majority are never seen twice, so the tables never fill in. the classical workaround is abstraction : hand-design buckets that lump "similar" hands and market states into one information set, solve the shrunken game, map back. it works (it powered early poker bots) but it is lossy, brittle, and demands deep domain expertise to design the buckets. deep cfr's thesis: don't hand-design buckets — let a neural network learn to generalise across information sets automatically. 💡 the one-line idea deep cfr is to cfr what deep \(q\)-learning is to tabular \(q\)-learning, and what ppo's policy net is to a tabular policy: replace the giant lookup tables with function approximators. instead of storing a regret number per \((i,a)\) cell, train a network \(r_\psi(i)\) that predicts the regret vector from features of \(i\). two hands that play similarly get similar predictions without either being visited — generalisation does the work abstraction used to. the two networks deep cfr trains (per player \(p\)) two kinds of network, replacing the two cfr tables: advantage / regret network \(r_\psi^p(i,a)\). takes the features of an information set, outputs a value per legal action that approximates the cumulative counterfactual regret (more precisely its iteration-average — regret matching is scale-invariant, so predicting the average is equivalent and numerically nicer). the current strategy at any \(i\) is just regret matching on this network's output : \(\sigma(i,a)\propto r_\psi^p(i,a)^{+}\). average-strategy network \(\pi_\phi(i,a)\). approximates the running average strategy \( ar\sigma\) — the object that actually converges to nash (the §8 pitfall: it's the average , never the last iterate). this network is the final deliverable you deploy to play figgie. the training loop because traversing the whole tree is impossible, deep cfr rides on external-sampling monte-carlo cfr (§8): sample chance and the opponents' moves, enumerate only the traverser's actions. each cfr iteration \(t\): traverse. for the traverser \(p\), run \(k\) external-sampling traversals. the strategy used at each visited \(i\) is regret matching on the current advantage network \(r_\psi^p\). collect regret targets. at every information set visited, compute the sampled instantaneous counterfactual regret vector \(	ilde r^t(i,\cdot)\), and push the sample \( ig(i,\ t,\ 	ilde r^t(i,\cdot) ig)\) into a fixed-size advantage memory \(\mathcal m_v^p\) using reservoir sampling (so the buffer always holds a uniform sample of everything seen so far, despite finite size). collect strategy targets. push \( ig(i,\ t,\ \sigma^t(i,\cdot) ig)\) into a separate strategy memory \(\mathcal m_\pi\) — the raw material for the average-strategy network. retrain from scratch. re-initialise \(r_\psi^p\) and fit it (regression) to the regrets in \(\mathcal m_v^p\), weighting each sample by its iteration \(t\) (this is the linear cfr weighting — later iterations matter more). training from scratch each time is deliberate : the target is a cumulative quantity that shifts, and warm-starting biases the fit. after \(t\) iterations, train \(\pi_\phi\) once on \(\mathcal m_\pi\) (again \(t\)-weighted) to recover \( ar\sigma\). that network is your approximate nash strategy. # deep cfr, one player's iteration (external sampling) for t in range(t): for p in players: for k in range(k): # k sampled traversals traverse(root, traverser=p, strategy= lambda i: regret_match(r_psi[p](i)), # σ from net on_visit= lambda i, r_hat: m_v[p]. add ((i, t, r_hat)), # reservoir on_strat= lambda i, sigma: m_pi. add ((i, t, sigma))) r_psi[p] = train_from_scratch(m_v[p], weight_by=t) # linear cfr avg_strategy_net = train_from_scratch(m_pi, weight_by=t) # ← deploy this 🃏 what this buys you on figgie you never enumerate figgie's information sets. you play sampled games; at each decision the advantage network reads your hand + market history and outputs a regret estimate per action ( buy ♥ , quote_ask(♦,8) , …); regret matching turns that into the mixed strategy you actually play; and the visited situations become regression targets. a hand you have never held gets a sensible regret prediction because it looks like hands the net has seen — the generalisation that hand-built abstraction could only approximate. crucially, you can reuse the very same figgietransformer trunk from the ppo setup: same token-sequence encoder of the game history, but with a regret-regression head and a cfr target instead of a clipped policy-gradient loss. how deep cfr differs from ppo/grpo — and why it's worth the trouble ppo / grpo deep cfr what the net predicts action probabilities (& value) per-action regret (+ a 2nd net for avg strategy) training target clipped advantage-weighted gradient regression onto sampled counterfactual regret strategy comes from the net's softmax, directly regret matching on the net's output what converges the current weights (no game-theoretic guarantee) the average-strategy net → nash (with approx. error) hidden information encoded into history, implicit first-class via information sets & counterfactual values memory machinery a rollout buffer, reused a few epochs reservoir-sampled advantage & strategy memories ⚠ the catches deep cfr's convergence guarantee is now approximate — it degrades with the networks' regression error, so a badly-fit advantage net no longer provably approaches nash. it needs large reservoir memories and a full network re-fit every iteration (expensive). and the two-network bookkeeping (advantage + average-strategy, per player) is heavier than a single ppo policy. these costs are why later work compressed it: single deep cfr drops the separate average-strategy net; dream and armac fold the regret estimate into an actor–critic-style single-trajectory method, pulling deep cfr much closer to the policy-gradient world you already know. ✦ deep cfr in one breath run monte-carlo cfr, but store the regrets it produces as training data and learn a network to predict regret from any information set — so generalisation replaces hand-built abstraction, and a game far too large to tabulate (like real figgie) becomes tractable. you keep cfr's prize — convergence toward an unexploitable strategy — while gaining the scalability of the deep nets that power ppo. it is, quite literally, the bridge between the two families this document has been building toward. section 10 so which do you use for figgie? you now have three tools and a feel for their shapes. the honest answer is that they occupy different niches, and a strong figgie agent might borrow from all of them. the case for ppo / grpo (what figgierl does) the action space is large and structured, the state is a long history best handled by a transformer, and you want to scale with compute. policy gradients eat all of this natively. ppo gives you stable, sample-reusable updates; the group structure of grpo cancels the dominant variance source (the deal). with a self-play snapshot pool to keep the opponent distribution rich, this is the pragmatic, scalable path — and it's exactly the design in figgie/ . its weakness is principled: it chases a moving target and carries no guarantee of unexploitability. the case for cfr if your goal is a provably hard-to-exploit strategy and you're willing to abstract figgie down to a tractable game, cfr (cfr+/mccfr, or deep cfr to keep the neural generalisation) targets the right object — nash — and treats hidden information as a first-class citizen rather than something to encode and hope about. its weakness is equally principled: full figgie's game tree forces heavy abstraction, and "unexploitable" is not the same as "maximally exploits weak opponents." 💡 the honest synthesis they optimise different things. ppo/grpo maximise expected profit against the opponents you actually train on — superb for exploiting a known field, at the risk of being exploited back. cfr minimises worst-case exploitability — a safety floor, sometimes leaving money on the table against bad players. the frontier of game ai (e.g. rebel, player-of-games) combines them: cfr-style equilibrium reasoning for soundness, deep nets and gradient learning for scale. for figgierl today, ppo+gae with grpo-style grouped self-play is the right engine; understanding cfr tells you precisely what guarantee that engine is and isn't giving you. the whole document on one page method one-line idea advantage estimate key risk reinforce reinforce actions by return-to-go \(g_t\) (raw mc) crippling variance + baseline subtract expected return \(g_t-b(s_t)\) need a good \(b\) actor–critic learn the baseline as \(v_\phi\) \(\delta_t\) / gae biased if critic wrong ppo reuse data safely via clipped ratio gae \((\gamma,\lambda)\) tuning, critic quality grpo group mean replaces the critic \((r_i- ar r)/\sigma\) coarse credit; \(g\) rollouts cfr play proportional to regret; average it counterfactual regret tree size → abstraction section 11 references & where to go next williams (1992) , simple statistical gradient-following algorithms for connectionist rl — the original reinforce. sutton, mcallester, singh & mansour (2000) , policy gradient methods for rl with function approximation — the policy gradient theorem. schulman et al. (2016) , high-dimensional continuous control using generalized advantage estimation — gae. schulman et al. (2019) — deep cfr. spinning up in deep rl (openai) — the best hands-on companion for §§3–6. written for the figgierl project · examples mirror the figgie/ library (transformer actor–critic, ppo + gae, group-structured self-play, muon optimiser). the fastest way to cement all of this: open figgie/train.py and match every term in ppo_loss_batched to a line in §§5–7.

title: Can a network tell you which examples it has already memorized?
desc: A frozen random network gives a surprisingly clean 'I've seen this enough' signal. We use it to down-weight memorized examples and re-balance a lop-sided dataset, with no labels.
text: experiment · generalization can a network tell you which examples it has already memorized? nerdsniped • june 2026 • ~20 min read • mnist & cifar-100 here is a shower-thought that refused to leave me alone. a neural network overfits by continuing to push the loss down on examples it has already gotten right — grinding a training point from 99% confidence to 99.9% confidence, learning nothing that generalizes, just memorizing harder. if only the network could raise its hand and say "i've seen this one enough, stop wasting gradient on it" , we could spend that capacity elsewhere and maybe overfit less. the obvious objection: to know an example is "already learned" you'd compute its loss, which needs its label, and now you're just doing hard-example mining with extra steps. but what if the signal didn't need labels at all? what if a network could measure its own familiarity with an input — how many times it has effectively seen something like it — directly, cheaply, as a side effect of training? this post is the writeup of chasing that idea. it has a clean trick at its center (a frozen random network), a deliberately rigged experiment to test it, an honest scorecard, and — because staring at high-dimensional representations is the best part — a pair of interactive umap explorers you can poke at. spoiler, so you can calibrate: the familiarity signal is real and surprisingly clean, but turning it into free generalization is harder than it looks. that gap is the interesting part. 1 the familiarity signal: a frozen random network the trick comes from a 2018 rl paper, random network distillation (rnd), where it was used to make agents curious about novel states. it is almost suspiciously simple. take two small networks, both reading the same input $x$: $r$, a target with random weights that we freeze forever . it computes some arbitrary, fixed, nonlinear function $r(x)$. we never train it; it is just a fixed random landscape. $p$, a predictor that we train to imitate the target by minimizing $\lvert p(x) - r(x)\rvert^2$. now watch the prediction error $\lvert p(x)-r(x)\rvert^2$ as training proceeds. on inputs $p$ has seen many times, it has had ample opportunity to match $r$ there, so the error is small . on inputs that are rare or novel, $p$ has had little practice, so the error stays large . the error is a familiarity meter : low where the data is dense and well-trodden, high where it is sparse or new. and it costs nothing but a forward pass — no labels, no notion of "correct." why a random target? the target just needs to be a fixed, deterministic, sufficiently expressive function that $p$ has to actually learn rather than guess. a random net is the cheapest such oracle: there's nothing to "understand," so the only way for $p$ to lower its error on a region is to have spent gradient steps there. the error becomes a clean proxy for "how much have i trained on inputs like this." so here is the plan. train a classifier $m$ as usual, but down-weight each example by how familiar it is . familiar (memorized) examples get a small weight; novel ones keep a big one. per minibatch, the loss is $$\mathcal{l} \;=\; \underbrace{\tfrac1n\sum_i \lvert p_i - r_i\rvert^2}_{\text{train the predictor } p} \;+\; \underbrace{\tfrac1n\sum_i \operatorname{sg}\!\big[\lvert p_i - r_i\rvert^2\big]\cdot \ell_i(m)}_{\text{familiarity-weighted classifier loss}}$$ where $\ell_i(m)$ is the classifier's per-example cross-entropy and $\operatorname{sg}[\cdot]$ is stop-gradient (pytorch's .detach() ). three rules make this behave: $r$ is frozen. it is a fixed ruler; training it would move the goalposts. $p$ trains only through the first term. its input is detached from $m$, so no classifier gradient leaks into the familiarity meter. the weight is detached. the factor multiplying $\ell_i$ is a number, not a thing to optimize — it re-weights examples without contributing gradients of its own. crucially this is a per-example product, $\sum_i w_i\,\ell_i$, not (mean weight)·(mean loss). one implementation note that matters: the raw $\lvert p-r\rvert^2$ shrinks globally as $p$ learns, which would quietly turn down the classifier's whole learning rate. we don't care about the absolute scale, only the relative emphasis across examples, so each minibatch normalizes the weights to mean 1. that keeps the comparison against an unweighted baseline honest — same effective learning rate, only the within-batch tilt differs. 2 where should the meter look? not at pixels. the first version i built fed $r$ and $p$ the raw pixels. it worked, in the sense that the error did separate common from rare inputs — but the separation tracked pixel-space density , which is only loosely related to what the classifier has actually memorized. two mnist sevens can be far apart in pixel space yet identical to the model; a familiarity meter on pixels can't know that. so i moved the towers' eyes inward. they now read the classifier's own mid-network latent — the token stream halfway through the vision transformer, mean-pooled — detached so no gradient flows back. now "familiarity" is measured in the representation the model actually uses to decide, and it drifts as the model learns: as $m$ reshapes its latent space, $r$'s readout of that space moves too, and $p$ chases a slowly moving target. this one change is the difference between a meter on the world and a meter on the model's experience of the world. the setup. a small vit classifies the image. halfway up, a detached copy of the (mean-pooled) token stream is fed to two mlp towers: a frozen random target r and a predictor p trained to imitate it. their squared difference is the per-example familiarity weight that re-scales the classifier's loss. dashed arrows are stop-gradient. 3 a rigged-on-purpose experiment to test "down-weight what you've memorized," i want a setting where memorization and generalization visibly diverge, and where i know the right answer in advance so i can check the trick against an oracle. class imbalance is perfect for this. i built a deliberately lopsided mnist: a training pool of 10,000 images that is 91% the digit 8 (9,100 of them) and just 100 examples each of the other nine digits. the model will see eights constantly and memorize them to death, while starving on the rare digits. then i evaluate on a perfectly balanced test set (200 of every digit) and report macro accuracy — the unweighted mean over classes — because a model that simply always shouts "8" already scores 91% by the naive metric and 10% by the honest one. the classifier is a small from-scratch vit (7×7 patches → 16 tokens, 4 transformer blocks, ~139k parameters); each tower is a ~115k-parameter mlp reading the latent after block 2. no dropout, no weight decay — i want it to overfit so there is something to fix. then i run three training regimes that differ only in how examples are weighted: regime per-example weight uses labels? baseline 1 (uniform) — normal_weight 1/91 for eights, 1 otherwise yes (oracle) novelty-rnd detached $\lvert p-r\rvert^2$ no the middle one, normal_weight , is the oracle: classic inverse-frequency balancing that uses the labels to make every class contribute equal total weight (each of the 9,100 eights is worth $1/91$ of a rare example, so the eights collectively weigh the same as one rare digit). it is what good label-based re-balancing buys you — the target our label-free trick is trying to sneak up on. the baseline is the do-nothing control. novelty-rnd is the contender. 4 does the meter even fire? before asking whether the trick helps, the precondition: does the familiarity signal actually assign lower novelty to the over-memorized eights than to the starved rare digits? if $p$ predicts $r$ equally well everywhere, there's nothing to re-weight and the rest is moot. it fires, and cleanly. tracking the mean novelty separately for the majority and the rare classes, the two curves pull apart almost immediately and stay apart: by late training the rare digits carry 2.5× the novelty of the eights. the meter has, with no labels, discovered which class the model is drowning in. the familiarity meter separates the classes. mean $\lvert p-r\rvert^2$ per step (log scale), split by majority digit 8 vs the nine rare digits. the predictor matches the random target far better on the abundant eights, so they read as "familiar" (low novelty); the rare digits stay novel. no labels were used to produce this split. 5 the honest scorecard so the signal is there. does down-weighting by it actually help us generalize? here the story gets more nuanced — which is to say, more honest. first, the training curves. all three regimes drive train accuracy to ~100% (this is a memorization machine by construction). on the balanced test set, the baseline climbs, peaks, and then degrades as it overfits the eights — the classic overfitting signature. the oracle normal_weight sits clearly above everyone. novelty-rnd tracks just above the baseline and, notably, degrades less after the peak. macro test accuracy over training. labeled re-balancing (green) wins. the label-free novelty weighting (orange) rides just above the baseline (blue) and resists the post-peak slide, but does not approach the oracle. now the per-class breakdown, which is where the trick shows its hand. compared to the baseline, novelty-rnd lifts the rare digits and gently lowers the majority — it is re-balancing in exactly the right direction, landing partway between the baseline and the labeled oracle. it is doing the right thing; it is just doing a fraction of it. per-class balanced-test accuracy. digit 8 (the 91% majority) is the leftmost group's far value; the rest are the starved 1% classes. novelty-rnd (orange) sits between baseline and oracle on most rare digits while giving up a sliver of majority accuracy — re-balancing without ever seeing a class label. put the numbers in one place. i report final macro accuracy (after the overfitting has happened — the fairest "did it overfit less" measure), the mean rare-class accuracy, and the majority accuracy: regime final macro rare-class acc majority (8) acc best macro, 3 seeds baseline 0.667 0.631 0.99 0.691 normal_weight (oracle) 0.707 0.678 0.965 0.731 novelty-rnd 0.689 0.657 0.98 0.689 read this carefully, because it's a mixed verdict and both halves matter: the good: at the end of training, novelty-rnd beats the baseline's macro accuracy (0.689 vs 0.667) and rare-class accuracy (0.657 vs 0.631), purely from a label-free familiarity signal. it really does overfit the majority a bit less and spend more on the rare classes. the sobering: it recovers maybe half the gap to the labeled oracle, and when you take each method's best point across three seeds, novelty-rnd is statistically indistinguishable from the baseline (0.689 vs 0.691) while the oracle is clearly ahead (0.731). the signal is real; the free lunch is small and seed-dependent. the uncomfortable subtlety inverse-frequency weighting needs to perfectly cancel the 91:1 ratio to balance the classes. our novelty ratio is about 2.5:1. so the meter knows the eights are over-represented, but it under-states by how much — familiarity saturates long before frequency does. a meter that says "you've seen this plenty" can't easily distinguish "plenty" from "ninety-one times too much." that ceiling, more than anything, is why the trick re-balances in the right direction but only partway. 6 the fun part: a umap explorer numbers are a summary; representations are the thing. so here is the part i actually built this for — an interactive map of how these 1,500 balanced-test images lay out in the model's representation, layer by layer. two dropdowns: layer sweeps from the patch embedding up through the four transformer blocks, then into the side towers' own activations ( p:hidden1 → p:out , and the frozen r ). you're watching the representation sharpen — and you can literally see where the towers' world looks nothing like the classifier's. color by recolors the same points by class label or by a low-level image statistic (brightness, ink, stroke energy…). it's a quick test of whether a cluster is "about the digit" or secretly "about the brightness." and hover any point to see the actual image . go find the majority blob (digit 8, outlined in black); watch it tighten into a dense island in the deeper blocks while the starved classes stay diffuse. mnist representation explorer. umap (cosine, standardized, pca-50) of each layer's embedding. the black-outlined points are the 91% majority (digit 8). notice how, in the classifier blocks, the eights condense into a tight cluster — that tightness is the low-novelty region the meter down-weights — while the r tower (a frozen random map) shows no class structure at all, exactly as it should. 7 does it survive a real dataset? cifar-100 mnist is a toy; structure there is almost free. the honest test is a dataset where the model has to fight for its representation. so i ran the identical pipeline on cifar-100 , with one fine class — sunflower — blown up to 90% of a 20,000-image pool and the other 99 classes sharing the scraps (~20 examples each). bigger vit (~0.83m parameters, 6 blocks), same three regimes, same balanced-test macro accuracy over 100 classes (where "always predict the majority" scores just 1%). this regime is brutal — 99 classes with a couple dozen examples each — so absolute numbers are low across the board; what matters is the relative picture. regime best macro rare-class acc majority acc baseline 8.9% 7.9% 90% normal_weight (oracle) 8.6% 7.7% 52% novelty-rnd 8.8% 8.0% 86% this is the most instructive failure in the whole project. with ~20 images per rare class, the 99 minority classes are essentially unlearnable , so every method is pinned near the ~9% macro floor. the novelty meter fires harder than on mnist — a 5.6× majority/rare split — yet it buys nothing, because there is no generalization to recover. watch what re-balancing does in this regime: the labeled oracle dutifully drives loss toward the rare classes, but with nothing learnable there it mostly just throws away majority accuracy (sunflower crashes from 90% to 52%) for no macro gain. novelty-rnd, gentle by construction, keeps far more of the majority (86%) and edges everyone on rare-class accuracy — but in truth it is a wash at the floor. the lesson is sharp: a strong familiarity signal is necessary but not sufficient ; re-weighting can move accuracy around, it cannot conjure generalization the data does not contain. cifar-100 macro test accuracy. all three are pinned near the ~9% floor: with ~20 examples per rare class there is nothing to learn. the labeled oracle (green) does not lead here — it drives loss toward the rare classes but mostly sacrifices majority accuracy. novelty-rnd (orange) stays gentle and tracks the baseline. per-class accuracy, method vs baseline. each point is one of the 100 classes; above the diagonal means the method beats the baseline on that class. the star is the majority class. the star is the majority class — note how far it falls for the oracle (green), traded away for rare classes that 20 examples cannot teach. most rare classes barely move for either method; the headroom simply is not there. and the same explorer, now in glorious color — try coloring by avg color or saturation and you'll often find the early layers organize by palette before they organize by category. hover to confirm with the actual photo. cifar-100 representation explorer. the dominant class is outlined; switch the color by dropdown to see how much of the early-layer geometry is explained by low-level image statistics versus class. the frozen r tower again shows no class structure — its job is to be a fixed ruler, not a classifier. 8 what i think is going on here's my read after staring at all of this. the familiarity meter works — genuinely, label-free, and it lives in the right space once you point it at the model's latents instead of the pixels. you can see it fire, you can watch the majority condense into the low-novelty island, you can measure the 2.5× split. as a diagnostic for "what has this model over-memorized," i'd reach for it again. but as a lever for generalization, it's a blunt instrument, for two reasons that i think are fundamental rather than fixable with more tuning: familiarity saturates; frequency doesn't. the predictor's error bottoms out once an example is "learned enough," so the meter compresses a 91× frequency gap into a ~2.5× novelty gap. it can tell you the eights are over-represented but badly under-counts by how much — and re-balancing needs the magnitude, not just the sign. down-weighting the majority is not the same as helping the minority. the oracle works because it actively re-allocates total loss mass to the rare classes. detached novelty only ever shrinks weights; with the mean-1 normalization it tilts within a batch, but it can't manufacture the aggressive minority emphasis that inverse-frequency weighting gets for free from labels. none of this kills the idea. it sharpens it. the version i'd build next stops treating novelty as a weight and starts treating it as a signal to act on : a curriculum that resamples toward high-novelty examples (which can over-emphasize the minority, breaking the saturation ceiling), or a calibration step that maps the saturating novelty back onto something closer to a frequency. the meter is good; the actuator is what's weak. the honest one-liner a frozen random network gives you a clean, label-free read on what your model has memorized. using that read to down-weight memorized examples nudges generalization in the right direction but recovers only part of what label-based balancing gets — because "i've seen this enough" saturates long before "i've seen this 91× too often." 9 takeaways a frozen-random target + a chasing predictor turns "how much have i trained on inputs like this" into a cheap, label-free number. point it at the model's mid-layer latents , not the pixels. in a 91%-majority mnist it discovers the over-memorized class on its own (2.5× novelty split) and re-balances training in the right direction — final macro 0.667 → 0.689 — but lands only partway to a labeled oracle (0.707), and the gain washes out into seed noise at the per-method best. the same qualitative story holds on cifar-100. at ~20 examples per rare class the minority is essentially unlearnable, so re-balancing can't manufacture macro accuracy — the labeled oracle even hurts by over-correcting the majority (90%→52%), while the gentle novelty weighting does least harm, and the meter fires hardest of all (5.6×). the bottleneck is that familiarity saturates : it nails the sign of the imbalance but understates its magnitude. the next iteration should resample on the signal rather than merely scale by it. reproducibility. everything is from-scratch pytorch — a small vit classifier, two mlp towers on its mid-layer latent, adam, no augmentation or regularization. mnist and cifar-100 are downloaded directly (no torchvision). the novelty weight is detached and mean-1 normalized per batch; $r$ is frozen; $p$ trains only on $\lvert p-r\rvert^2$. the umap explorers project each layer's embedding with cosine umap after standardization and pca-50, and embed the original images as base64 for hover. two companion notebooks reproduce every figure on this page.
